--- Page 1 ---
What is a work system? (M1.3)
Woojin Park
Life Enhancing Technology Laboratory
SNU Department of Industrial Engineering

--- Page 2 ---
What Is a Work System?
• To understand work system design, we must first understand:
– What a system is in engineering terms
– Why systems thinking differs from component thinking
• Systems thinking focuses on interaction, not isolated parts.

--- Page 3 ---
What Is a System?
• A system is:
– A bounded, goal-oriented set of interrelated elements
– That transforms inputs into outputs
– Through structured interaction
– While maintaining dynamic internal states
• Five key characteristics:
1. Purpose — Exists to achieve an objective
2. Boundary — Distinguishes system from environment
3. Inputs/Outputs — Transforms resources into results
4. Interdependence — Elements influence one another
5. State & Feedback — Internal conditions evolve over time
• Work systems are open systems:
– Exchange information, materials, and energy with environment

--- Page 4 ---
Systems Perspective: Emergence
• From a systems perspective:
– Performance is not located in a single part
– It emerges from structured interaction
• Emergence means:
– Outcomes arise from how elements work together
– Not from any single component alone
• This concept is foundational for understanding work systems.

--- Page 5 ---
From Systems to Work Systems
• Major Work System Frameworks
– Alter’s Work System Framework
– SEIPS Model
– ISO 6385 Ergonomics Framework

--- Page 6 ---
Alter’s Work System Framework (Managerial Lens)
• Core elements:
– Customers
– Products/services
– Processes and activities
– Participants
– Information
– Technologies
– Environment
– Infrastructure
– Strategy
• Core insight:
– Systems should not be analyzed as just IT
– Technology is only one component of a broader work system

--- Page 7 ---
SEIPS Model (Human Factors Lens)
• SEIPS = Systems Engineering Initiative for Patient Safety
• Core components:
– Person(s)
– Tasks
– Tools/technology
– Physical environment
– Organizational conditions
• These influence:
– Processes
– Outcomes (safety, quality, well-being)
• Contribution:
– Explicit link between system design and safety
– Emphasizes interaction and variability

--- Page 8 ---
SEIPS Model (Human Factors Lens)

--- Page 9 ---
ISO 6385 (Normative Ergonomic Lens)
• Focus:
– Balanced work system design considering:
• Human capabilities
• Technical requirements
• Organizational structure
• Contribution:
– Performance and well-being must be jointly optimized
– Formalizes socio-technical balance

--- Page 10 ---
Convergence and Emergence
• Across frameworks, common components include:
– Participants
– Tasks/processes
– Tools/technology
– Information
– Organizational/environmental context
– Outputs/customers
• Shared assumption:
– System performance is emergent
• Examples:
– Productivity → task coordination
– Safety → demand-capability alignment
– Quality → stable interactions
– Motivation → task structure & feedback
• Improvement requires systemic redesign.

--- Page 11 ---
Integrated Definition of Work System
• A work system is an open socio-technical system
• Humans and/or machines perform interdependent processes
• Using resources to produce value-generating outputs

--- Page 12 ---
Industrial Engineering

--- Page 13 ---
Distinguishing Features of Work Systems
• Work systems are:
– Socio-technical — Human and technical elements interact
– Value-oriented — Outputs matter to stakeholders
– Organizationally embedded — Roles, rules, and authority shape 
behavior

--- Page 14 ---
Scientific Management in System Terms
• Scientific management can be reinterpreted as:
– An early attempt at work system redesign
• Taylor aimed to:
– Reduce variability
– Standardize methods
– Clarify roles
– Separate planning and execution
– Establish measurement and feedback
• System interpretation:
– Control internal state
– Stabilize performance through structured interaction
• Scientific management = early work system engineering

--- Page 15 ---
Mini-Summary
• A work system is:
– An open socio-technical system
– Organized around purposeful activity
– Designed to create value
• Performance emerges from interaction among:
– People
– Tasks
– Tools
– Information
– Organizational context
• Systems thinking shifts focus from isolated components to 
structured interaction and feedback
